| 2026 Q1 | 24,347 | 8,179 | **34%** | $1.00M | Innovairrs & Co. |

Two things a reporter can say with confidence:

1. **A third of the ledger has always been blank.** The share of filings with *no dollar figure at
   all* sits at 34–36% every single year from 2022 through 2026. This is not a glitch or a bad year —
   it's how the system has continuously operated. Anyone totalling "lobbying spend" from this data is
   working with a third of the records missing their central number.

2. **Nothing enforces an upper sanity bound, and 2025 proves it.** In every normal year the biggest
   single filing tops out around $1.5–3.5 million. Then in 2025 one filing reports **$20 million** —
   roughly **6× the largest legitimate filing ever seen** in four years — and it posted, published,
   and mirrored into the House system with nothing flagging it. The ceiling didn't rise because
   lobbying got bigger; it rose because a fabricated number met no check.

So the vulnerability (no validation of who files or what they claim) is **permanent and structural.**
The specific $80M distortion is a 2025 event, but the open door that let it happen has been open the
whole time. *(Next step to make this airtight: repeat the null-rate for the House dataset and confirm
the same pattern pre-2022 if you extend the corpus.)*

Sources: all figures reproducible from the built store; per-year totals and the blank-income counts
above were computed directly from the Senate filings (`data/senate/<year>/filings/…`).

---

## Question 2: Is filing a false report actually illegal — or does it just sit there?

**I read the real statutes rather than going from memory.** Short answer: **on paper a knowing false
filing probably is illegal, but the law is built and used to chase people who *don't file* — not
people who file false content — so in practice a fabricated figure faces essentially no consequence
and simply sits in the public record. "Illegal in principle, effectively unenforced."** And there's an
important intent wrinkle that cuts in the filer's favour.

Here's the actual law, in plain terms.

### The lobbying law's own penalties — 2 U.S.C. § 1606
*(read at law.cornell.edu/uscode/text/2/1606, as-of 2026-07-15)*

- **Civil (§ 1606(a)):** someone who **"knowingly fails to… comply with any… provision of this
  chapter"** can be hit with a **civil fine up to $200,000.** Filing a report you know to be false is
  arguably a knowing failure to comply with the requirement to file accurate reports.
- **Criminal (§ 1606(b)):** someone who **"knowingly and corruptly fails to comply"** can be
  **imprisoned up to 5 years.** Higher bar — note the word *corruptly*.

So the lobbying statute does contain teeth for false filings. But look at how enforcement is wired.

### How it's actually enforced — 2 U.S.C. § 1605
*(read at law.cornell.edu/uscode/text/2/1605, as-of 2026-07-15)*

The Secretary of the Senate and Clerk of the House are told to *"review, and where necessary verify
and inquire to ensure the accuracy, completeness, and timeliness"* of filings. But the machinery that
follows is built entirely around **non-compliance** in the sense of missing/defective filings:

1. notify the filer **in writing** they may be non-compliant, then
2. only if they **fail to respond within 60 days**, refer them to the **U.S. Attorney for D.C.**

There is no proactive fraud-detection unit, no audit of dollar figures, no automatic flag when a
single client reports 6× the largest filing in the country. The Secretary/Clerk are administrators,
not investigators, and the referral pipe leads to one over-loaded prosecutor's office. In practice
the enforcement that does happen is overwhelmingly against people who **didn't file at all** — not
people who filed fiction. That's why an $80M fabrication can sit live on lda.senate.gov indefinitely.

### The general false-statements law — 18 U.S.C. § 1001
*(read at law.cornell.edu/uscode/text/18/1001, as-of 2026-07-15)*

This is the government-wide "lying to the feds" statute (up to 5 years). It normally applies to the
executive branch, but **§ 1001(c)(1)** extends it to legislative-branch matters for, among other
things, *"a document required by law, rule, or regulation to be submitted to… any office or officer
within the legislative branch."* An LDA report — filed with the Secretary of the Senate / Clerk of the
House — plausibly fits that description, so a **knowingly and willfully** false filing could in
principle be reached by § 1001. Whether courts actually apply § 1001 to LDA filings is not
well-settled and should be confirmed with a lawyer.

### The catch that matters most — and why it protects the filer, not the system

Every one of these hooks requires a **culpable state of mind**: § 1606(a) "knowingly," § 1606(b)
"knowingly and **corruptly**," § 1001 "knowingly and **willfully**." These words mean the government
must prove the filer **knew the statement was false and intended to deceive.** A person who *sincerely*
(however mistakenly) believes her "nation" is genuinely owed the money may simply **not have that
intent** — which could put her outside these criminal statutes entirely.

That is exactly why the honest story points at the system, not the individual:

- If she filed sincerely, **the law may not treat her as a criminal at all** — yet the false $80M
  still entered the official record and still distorted the national total. The failure isn't her
  culpability; it's that **nothing in the system stops or corrects the entry regardless of intent.**
- If she filed knowing it was false, then on paper she likely broke § 1606 (and maybe § 1001) — **and
  still nothing happened**, because no one detects, notices, or refers false content. Either way the
  spotlight lands on the absent gatekeeper.

**Verdict for the editor/lawyer:** *possible violation of 2 U.S.C. § 1606 (and possibly 18 U.S.C.
§ 1001), but effectively unenforced — the statute's own machinery is aimed at non-filers, and the
intent requirement makes a sincere-belief filer hard to reach.* This is an assessment for a lawyer,
not a conclusion; do not assert that anyone broke the law.

---

## What this means for the story

You can now say all of the following, sourced:

- The disclosure system has **no gatekeeper**, and this is **structural** — a third of filings have
  lacked a dollar figure every year for at least four years, and there is no check that would catch a
  fabricated figure 6× larger than any real one.
- When a fabrication does appear, it **silently distorts the totals** everyone relies on (the 2025
  fake = 2.78% of all reported US lobbying income).
- The law **does** contain penalties for false filings, but they are **aimed at non-filers and
  effectively never used against false content**, and the intent requirement means even a knowing
  case is hard — so a false $80M figure **just sits there.**
- The system-level fix questions write themselves: should the Secretary/Clerk validate filings, cap or
  query implausible figures, or flag content the way the IRS or SEC screen returns?

**Before publishing:** confirm the enforcement reality on the record (Secretary of the Senate's Office
of Public Records; DOJ's semiannual LDA enforcement reports under § 1605(b) — these show how few
actions are ever brought); have a lawyer confirm whether § 1001(c) reaches LDA filings; and give every
named party, including the filer, a fair chance to comment. Keep the framing on the system; treat the
filer as a person who may sincerely hold unusual beliefs, not a villain.

---

### Source index
- LDA penalties: **2 U.S.C. § 1606** — https://www.law.cornell.edu/uscode/text/2/1606 (as-of 2026-07-15)
- LDA enforcement/review duties: **2 U.S.C. § 1605** — https://www.law.cornell.edu/uscode/text/2/1605 (as-of 2026-07-15)
- False statements: **18 U.S.C. § 1001** (see § 1001(c) legislative-branch limit) — https://www.law.cornell.edu/uscode/text/18/1001 (as-of 2026-07-15)
- Year-by-year blank-rate + largest-filing table: computed from `data/senate/<year>/filings/filings_<year>.json`.
- The fabricated filing itself: `data/senate/2025/filings/filings_2025.json#4d5b1cb0-0971-43b8-958b-d260e2be8af1`.

content.text:
Both pinned down. The full memo is above; here's the plain-English version.

**The hole is structural, not a 2025 blip.** I re-ran the two measures for every year. The blank-filing rate is essentially flat — **36%, 36%, 35%, 35%, 34%** from 2022 through 2026 — so roughly a third of the ledger has *always* lacked a dollar figure. And in every normal year the largest single filing tops out around $1.5–3.5M; only in 2025 does it leap to Loc Nation's $20M — about 6× the biggest legitimate filing ever seen — with nothing catching it. So you can say confidently: the no-gatekeeper vulnerability is permanent, and 2025 is just when someone walked through the open door.

**Is a false filing illegal? Illegal in principle, but effectively unenforced — and I read the real statutes, not my memory.** The lobbying law's own penalty section (2 U.S.C. § 1606) does have teeth: a *knowing* false filing can draw a civil fine up to $200,000, and a *knowing and corrupt* one up to 5 years in prison. The general false-statements law (18 U.S.C. § 1001) might also reach it, via a legislative-branch carve-out that plausibly covers LDA reports. But two things gut it in practice:

- **The enforcement machinery (§ 1605) is built for non-filers, not false content.** The Secretary of the Senate / Clerk of the House send a written notice, give a 60-day cure window, then refer to a single overloaded U.S. Attorney's office. There's no audit of dollar figures, no fraud detection. So a fabricated $80M just sits live on the Senate site.
- **Every hook requires intent to deceive** ("knowingly," "corruptly," "willfully"). A filer who *sincerely* believes the money is owed may not meet that bar at all.

That intent point is actually why your instinct to keep the focus on the system is the right one: if she filed sincerely, the law may not treat her as a criminal — yet the fake number still entered the record and still distorted the national total. If she filed knowingly, she likely broke § 1606 — and *still* nothing happened. Either way, the spotlight lands on the absent gatekeeper, not the person.

Before this goes to anyone, three things to close out (all in the memo): confirm the enforcement reality on the record — DOJ's own semiannual LDA enforcement reports under § 1605(b) will show how few actions are ever brought; have a lawyer confirm whether § 1001 actually reaches LDA filings; and give every named party, including the filer, a fair chance to comment.

Want me to pull those DOJ semiannual enforcement numbers next to quantify "effectively never enforced," or start shaping this into a draft?

Sources: 2 U.S.C. § 1606, § 1605, and 18 U.S.C. § 1001 (law.cornell.edu, read today); year-by-year figures computed from the Senate filings; the filing itself at `filings_2025.json#4d5b1cb0…`.

I found comprehensive information about GAO's lobbying disclosure act compliance audits and U.S. Attorney enforcement efforts. Here are the key findings:

## GAO Annual Compliance Audits

The Lobbying Disclosure Act of 1995, as amended, includes a provision for GAO to annually audit the extent of lobbyists' compliance with the act. The most recent report (2024) is GAO's 18th annual review under the provision.

## U.S. Attorney Enforcement and Settlements

Civil and criminal enforcement actions for LDA violations can include complaints, lawsuits, settlements, civil penalties, and prosecutions. However, enforcement activity has been limited. As of December 2024, the Department of Justice has not brought any prosecutions related to nondisclosure of relevant crimes under the Justice Against Corruption on K Street Act of 2018 (JACK Act) since the law's requirements went into effect.

Historical settlement examples include: Since 2007, the U.S. Attorney's Office has entered into negotiated settlements three times — once in 2011 for $45,000 and twice in 2012 for $50,000 and $30,000.

## Noncompliance Referrals

USAO received 3,566 referrals from both the Secretary of the Senate and the Clerk of the House for failure to comply with LD-2 reporting requirements cumulatively for filing years 2015 through 2024.

## Chronic Offender Enforcement

USAO considers lobbyists chronic offenders if they repeatedly fail to file LD-2 and LD-203 reports but are allegedly lobbying, and USAO has received more than 10 referrals for them. USAO officials track chronic offenders in their database and send all chronic offenders "chronic offender letters," which identify all reporting periods where the lobbyist remains noncompliant.

file.content:
USAO received 3,566 referrals from both the Secretary of
the Senate and the Clerk of the House for failure to comply with LD-2 reporting
requirements cumulatively for filing years 2015 through 2024. Figure 11 shows
the number and status of the referrals received and the number of enforcement
actions taken by USAO to bring lobbyists into compliance. Enforcement actions
include letters, emails, and telephone calls.

Figure 11: Status of Lobbying Disclosure Act Referrals for
Failure to File Quarterly Lobbying Disclosure (LD-2) Reports, 2015—2024 (as of
December 2024)

![](https://files.gao.gov/reports/GAO-25-107523/d25107523_web_files/image012.jpg)

Note: According to Secretary of the Senate and Clerk of
the House staff, they send most referrals to the U.S. Attorney’s Office for the
District of Columbia within 6 months of the filing deadline. However, some
referrals fall outside this 6-month period. The number of referrals pending
further action and the number of referrals now compliant may not sum to the
total number of referrals, shown in parentheses. This difference occurs because
referrals for lobbyists or clients who are deceased or no longer in business
are included in the total number of referrals but are not displayed in the bar
chart.

According to USAO data, about 36 percent (1,290 of 3,566)
of the total LD-2 referrals received were closed as in compliance as of
December 2024, because lobbyists filed their outstanding reports before or
after the Department of Justice contacted them. About 63 percent (2,244 of
3,566) of LD-2 referrals were pending further action because USAO could not
locate the lobbyists, did not receive a response from the lobbyists after an
enforcement action, or planned to conduct additional research to determine if it
can locate the lobbyist. The remaining 32 LD-2 referrals (about 1 percent) did
not require action or were suspended because the lobbyist or client was no
longer in business, or the individual lobbyist was deceased.

#### Status of LD-203 Enforcement Efforts

Both lobbying firms and the individual lobbyists with each
firm are required to file LD-203 reports, which disclose contributions to
federal political campaigns, among other details.[[35]](#_ftn35 "") USAO categorizes referrals for
failure to file LD-203s into two types:

1.LD-203(R) referrals for lobbying firms or organizations with in-house
lobbyists that failed to file LD-203s disclosing the firms’ or organizations’
contributions, as required.

2.LD-203 referrals for lobbying firms or organizations with in-house
lobbyists whose employees failed to file LD-203s disclosing the individual
lobbyists’ contributions, as required.

Cumulatively from 2015 through 2024, USAO received 2,000
LD-203(R) referrals. As of December 2024, USAO closed about 40 percent (790 of
2,000) of these LD-203(R) referrals as in compliance because the lobbying firms
filed their outstanding reports. About 60 percent (1,190 of 2,000) of the
referrals were pending further action. The remaining 20 referrals (1 percent)
were suspended because the lobbyist or client was no longer in business, or the
lobbyist was deceased.[[36]](#_ftn36 "") Figure 12 shows the number and status of LD-203(R) referrals received and the
number of enforcement actions taken by USAO to bring lobbying firms into
compliance.

Figure 12: Status of Lobbying Disclosure Act LD-203(R)
Referrals for Lobbying Firms’ Failure to File Contribution Reports, 2015—2024
(as of December 2024)

![](https://files.gao.gov/reports/GAO-25-107523/d25107523_web_files/image013.jpg)

Note: According to Secretary of the Senate and Clerk of
the House staff, they send most referrals to the U.S. Attorney’s Office for the
District of Columbia within 6 months of the filing deadline. However, some
referrals fall outside this 6-month period. The number of referrals pending
further action and the number of referrals now compliant may not sum to the
total number of referrals, shown in parentheses. This difference occurs because
referrals for lobbyists or clients who are deceased or no longer in business
are included in the total number of referrals but are not displayed in the bar
chart.

Because LD-203 referrals are addressed to lobbying firms
or organizations that employ individual lobbyists who failed to file LD-203s, a
single LD-203 referral may list multiple individual lobbyists. For example, if
two individual lobbyists employed by the same lobbying firm failed to file

content.subject: Scope + open case file
content.description: Set working folder, write brief.md from the wide-net scope, init case file.

content.subject: Build the lobbying data store
content.description: Run mapper ingest.py (senate, contributions, press all years; house key years) + resolve_entities.py into a SQLite store.

content.subject: Profile / orient the data
content.description: Run top-of-funnel aggregates and robodoig-style profiling to learn what's normal.

content.subject: Discover & rank leads
content.description: Run gatekeeper, anomaly, mismatch, coordination, say-vs-do scans; surface candidate stories.

content.subject: Cross-check top leads against ground truth
content.description: Verify each promising lead against source records via review.py and outside data.

content.subject: Write up the 2-3 strongest leads
content.description: Plain-language brief: what the story is, why it stands out, confirmed vs lead, sources.

content.subject: Dump and verify the raw Loc Nation filings
content.description: Print full verbatim JSON of the Senate filings + House XML; confirm fields, filer identity, registration.

content.subject: Reconstruct how it entered the public record
content.description: Trace filing types, dates, amendments; explain the LDA self-filing/e-file acceptance path.

content.subject: Scan for other fake/garbage filings
content.description: Systematic scan across years: absurd incomes, round numbers, junk registrant/client names, sovereign-citizen language, test filings.

content.subject: Measure distortion of real totals
content.description: Quantify how much Loc Nation + junk inflates 2025 totals, top-client rankings, and per-issue aggregates.

content.subject: Write plain-language verification memo
content.description: One-off vs systemic verdict, with sources shown throughout.

content.subject: Measure distortion + blank-filing rate for 2022-2024
content.description: Per-year: total reported income, largest single filing (outlier ceiling), null-income %, to show whether the no-gatekeeper hole is structural across years.

content.subject: Check the actual law on false LDA filings
content.description: Use checking-the-law skill to read the real LDA statute + penalty/false-statement provisions; determine illegal-but-unenforced vs not-a-violation.
content.activeForm: Checking the law on false filings

content.subject: Write the combined memo
content.description: Plain-language: structural-over-time finding + legal verdict, system-focused framing, sources shown.

content.subject: Find real LDA enforcement numbers
content.description: Locate GAO annual LDA compliance audits + USAO-DC enforcement record; extract counts of actions and whether any were for false content vs non-filing. Source everything or report honestly if unavailable.

content.text: Important detail now confirmed from the primary source: GAO says the "enforcement actions" it counts "include letters, emails, and telephone calls" — i.e., nudges to get non-filers to file, not penalties. And the entire referral system is about *failure to file*. Let me confirm the actual monetary-penalty/settlement history and the JACK Act prosecution count.

file.content:
#### Status of Civil and Criminal Enforcement Actions

USAO officials reported that, in 2024, the Department of
Justice took one civil enforcement action against a lobbyist. Specifically, the
department reached a civil settlement with a lobbyist that included a $65,000
penalty and an agreement that the lobbyist permanently retire from lobbying at
the federal level. USAO officials stated that they had identified this lobbyist
as a chronic offender. USAO officials did not report taking any criminal
enforcement actions in 2024. Civil and criminal enforcement actions for LDA
violations can include complaints, lawsuits, settlements, civil penalties, and
prosecutions.

#### Justice Against Corruption on K Street Act of 2018 Enforcement

USAO officials stated that, as of December 2024, the
Department of Justice has not brought any prosecutions related to nondisclosure
of relevant crimes under the Justice Against Corruption on K Street Act of 2018
(JACK Act) since the law’s requirements went into effect. USAO confirmed its
enforcement role regarding the JACK Act is the same as any other prosecution.
If the individual or organization has filed a lobbying disclosure report with
misrepresentations, USAO officials said they can initiate criminal prosecution
or impose civil penalties under the LDA.

### Agency Comments

Based on the search results, I found relevant information about GAO lobbying disclosure reports and U.S. Attorney civil settlements related to the Lobbying Disclosure Act (LDA) enforcement since 2007:

## Civil Settlements

Since the LDA was passed in 1995, the Office has settled with three lobbyists and collected civil penalties totaling about $47,000 (as of the 2010 GAO report). However, enforcement activity increased significantly after the Honest Leadership and Open Government Act of 2007 (HLOGA).

The U.S. Attorney's Office entered into negotiated settlements three times — twice in 2012 for $50,000 and $30,000 and once in 2011 for $45,000. Additionally, in December of 2013, the U.S. Attorney's Office secured a default judgment of $200,000 for LDA violations, and in August of 2015, the U.S. Attorney's Office announced a settlement with a lobbying firm that includes a $125,000 fine for missing LDA reports.

## LDA Enforcement History Since 2007

From 2015 through 2024, USAO received 3,566 referrals from both the Secretary of the Senate and the Clerk of the House for failure to comply with LD-2 reporting requirements. As of December 2024, about 36 percent of these referrals were closed as in compliance, and about 63 percent were pending further action.

input.content:
# "Effectively unenforced" — now backed by the government's own numbers

*You wanted to stand behind that phrase with a real record, not just my reading of the statute.
There is one, and it's the government's own auditor. Below: what the record shows, what's
primary vs. secondary, and what I could NOT establish so you don't overstate it.*

---

## The source: GAO audits LDA enforcement every year, by law

Congress requires the **Government Accountability Office (GAO)** — the federal government's own
nonpartisan watchdog — to audit lobbying-disclosure compliance **annually**. The most recent is the
**2024 report, GAO-25-107523** (GAO's 18th annual review). It reports, from official U.S. Attorney's
Office (USAO) data, exactly how enforcement works and how much of it happens. I read it directly.

## What the record actually shows

**1. The entire enforcement pipeline is about *failing to file* — not false content.**
Over the ten filing years **2015 through 2024**, the Secretary of the Senate and the Clerk of the
House referred to the U.S. Attorney for D.C.:

- **3,566 referrals** for failing to file quarterly **LD-2** activity reports, and
- **2,000 referrals** for failing to file **LD-203** contribution reports.

That's **~5,566 referrals in a decade, every one for non-filing** (a lobbyist who didn't file at
all, or filed late). None are for a filer who submitted a false number. *(GAO-25-107523, Figures 11–12
and surrounding text.)*

**2. Even the "enforcement actions" are mostly letters and phone calls.** GAO states plainly that the
enforcement actions USAO takes to bring lobbyists into compliance **"include letters, emails, and
telephone calls."** Of the 3,566 LD-2 referrals, about **36% (1,290)** eventually filed; about
**63% (2,244)** were still **pending** as of December 2024 — because USAO "could not locate the
lobbyists" or got no response. In other words, most referrals are never actually resolved.

**3. Actual penalties are vanishingly rare, and aimed at repeat non-filers.** In all of **2024**, the
Department of Justice took exactly **one** civil enforcement action under the LDA — a **$65,000
settlement** (plus a promise to retire from lobbying) against a lobbyist USAO had flagged as a
**"chronic offender"** (someone who **repeatedly failed to file**). It took **zero criminal**
enforcement actions that year. *(GAO-25-107523, "Status of Civil and Criminal Enforcement Actions.")*

**4. No criminal prosecutions for the disclosure-fraud provision — ever.** As of December 2024, DOJ
**had not brought a single prosecution** under the 2019 JACK Act (which requires lobbyists to disclose
certain criminal convictions) since it took effect. And GAO records **no** criminal LDA prosecutions
in its recent reviews. Notably, GAO records USAO confirming it *could* act on a filing "with
misrepresentations" — it just hasn't. *(GAO-25-107523.)*

**5. The longer historical picture is the same — a handful of civil settlements, all for missing
reports.** Earlier GAO reports and reputable legal write-ups document that in the years after 2007
the total known monetary enforcement amounts to a **small handful** of civil settlements/judgments,
each against a lobbyist who **failed to file** — for example negotiated settlements of **$45,000
(2011)**, **$50,000 and $30,000 (2012)**, a **$200,000 default judgment (Dec 2013)**, and a
**$125,000 settlement (Aug 2015, "for missing LDA reports")**. I'm flagging these as *illustrative and
partly from secondary sources* (see caveat below) — but every documented action I found is a
non-filing case, none for false content.

## The line you can stand behind

> Over the decade 2015–2024, the Secretary of the Senate and Clerk of the House sent the U.S.
> Attorney some **5,566 enforcement referrals under the lobbying-disclosure law — every one for
> failing to file, none for false content** — and the office's "enforcement actions" were largely
> letters, emails and phone calls, with about **63% of referrals still unresolved**. Actual penalties
> are rare and reserved for chronic non-filers (a single **$65,000** civil settlement in 2024, and
> **zero criminal prosecutions**). The government's own auditor documents **no** case of anyone being
> penalized for putting a false figure into the record. *(Source: GAO-25-107523, 2024 Lobbying
> Disclosure audit.)*

That is a sourced, defensible foundation for "effectively unenforced" — and it's stronger than the
phrase, because it shows the enforcement machine isn't just small, it's **pointed at a different
problem** (non-filers) than the one your story is about (false content).

## Straight talk — what I did NOT establish (don't overstate these)

- **I do not have a single authoritative all-time count** of "total LDA enforcement actions, 1996–
  present." GAO reports year-by-year; the 2015–2024 referral totals and the 2024 action count are
  solid and primary. The pre-2015 settlement examples are from older GAO reports and law-firm
  summaries and should be **re-verified against the underlying GAO reports** before printing specific
  dollar figures.
- **The truly authoritative all-time number exists** and is worth requesting: under **2 U.S.C.
  § 1605(b)**, the Attorney General must report to Congress **every six months** the aggregate number
  of LDA enforcement actions and any sentences. Those DOJ semiannual reports (to the Senate Homeland
  Security/Judiciary and House Judiciary committees) are the primary record; obtaining a run of them
  would let you state an exact cumulative figure with total confidence.
- **"None for false content" is true of everything I found, but is a negative** — best framed as "no
  documented case of enforcement for a false filing," and confirmed on the record with USAO and via
  the § 1605(b) reports before publication.

## Bottom line for the story

The "no gatekeeper" thesis is now supported on both ends: **nothing checks a filing on the way in**
(a fabricated $80M posted and distorted the national total), and **almost nothing happens after** —
the government's own auditor shows enforcement is rare, mostly nudge-letters to non-filers, with no
documented penalty ever for false content. You can write "effectively unenforced" and point to GAO.

---

### Sources
- **GAO-25-107523**, *2024 Lobbying Disclosure: Observations on Compliance with Requirements* (read
  in full): https://www.gao.gov/products/gao-25-107523 · full text: https://files.gao.gov/reports/GAO-25-107523/index.html
  — referral totals (3,566 LD-2; 2,000 LD-203(R), 2015–2024); "enforcement actions include letters,
  emails, and telephone calls"; 2024 single $65,000 civil settlement / zero criminal; no JACK Act
  prosecutions; USAO could act on misrepresentations.
- Historical settlement examples (illustrative; verify before printing): earlier GAO annual reports,
  e.g. **GAO-11-452** (2010) and **GAO-13-437** (2012); Holland & Knight, "DOJ Secures $200,000
  Lobbying Disclosure Act Penalty" (2013); Wiley, "Largest Settlement Reached for Lobbying Disclosure
  Act Violation" (2015).
- Authoritative all-time record to request: **2 U.S.C. § 1605(b)** DOJ semiannual enforcement reports
  to Congress.

By law, the **GAO audits lobbying-disclosure compliance every year**. Its 2024 report (GAO-25-107523), which I read directly, lays out the enforcement numbers from the U.S. Attorney's Office. What it shows:

- Over the decade **2015–2024**, the Secretary of the Senate and Clerk of the House sent the U.S. Attorney roughly **5,566 referrals** (3,566 for missing LD-2 activity reports + 2,000 for missing LD-203 contribution reports) — **every single one for failing to file, none for false content.**
- GAO says the "enforcement actions" the office takes are largely **"letters, emails, and telephone calls,"** and about **63% of referrals were still unresolved** as of December 2024 because they couldn't locate the lobbyist or got no response.
- Actual penalties are vanishingly rare and aimed at repeat non-filers: in all of **2024, exactly one** civil action — a **$65,000 settlement** against a "chronic offender" — and **zero criminal** actions.
- **No JACK Act prosecutions, ever.** GAO even notes the office says it *could* act on a filing "with misrepresentations" — it just never has.

So the sourced version of your line is stronger than the assertion: *over 2015–2024 the government received ~5,566 disclosure-enforcement referrals, all for non-filing, handled mostly by letters, with a single $65,000 civil settlement in 2024 and no criminal prosecutions — and no documented case of anyone ever being penalized for putting a false figure in the record.*

Two honesty caveats, so you don't overstate it:

- I could **not** find one authoritative all-time count of "total LDA actions since 1996." The 2015–2024 figures and the 2024 action are solid and primary; the older settlement examples ($45k in 2011, $200k default judgment in 2013, $125k in 2015, all for missing reports) are illustrative and partly from secondary sources — re-verify before printing specific dollar amounts.
- The authoritative cumulative record **does exist and is worth requesting**: under 2 U.S.C. § 1605(b), DOJ must report enforcement actions to Congress every six months. Getting a run of those would let you state an exact all-time number with total confidence.

Everything from this stage — the verification, the structural data, the legal read, and now the enforcement record — is saved as separate memos in your investigation folder. Want me to draft a records request for the DOJ § 1605(b) reports to lock down that all-time figure, or start pulling these four memos into a single publishable draft?

Sources: [GAO-25-107523 (2024 Lobbying Disclosure audit)](https://www.gao.gov/products/gao-25-107523) · [full text](https://files.gao.gov/reports/GAO-25-107523/index.html); historical examples from earlier GAO reports, [Holland & Knight (2013)](https://www.hklaw.com/en/insights/publications/2013/12/doj-secures-200000-lobbying-disclosure-act-penalty) and [Wiley (2015)](https://www.wiley.law/newsletter-Largest-Settlement-Reached-for-Lobbying-Disclosure-Act-Violation).

content.text: This is the kind of thing a fact-checker would pounce on, and it's important. Two things already surface: the $80M is rock-solid ($20M × 4 quarters, amendment-aware, and Loc reports no expenses). But **"biggest client in America" is measured only by *income* (fees paid to outside firms)** — and if you count *in-house* lobbying (the "expenses" field), the U.S. Chamber reports $70M, Realtors $54M, PhRMA $38M. So that claim needs correcting. Let me now pressure-test the distortion denominator and the blank-rate, which are the other soft spots.

import sqlite3

c.row_factory=sqlite3.Row
print("############ CLAIM 3: distortion % — test the denominator ############")
def dedup_sum(col,year):
    return c.execute(f"""with d as (select max({col}) v from senate_filings
      where filing_year=? and {col} is not null group by registrant_id,client_id,filing_period)
      select coalesce(sum(v),0) from d""",(year,)).fetchone()[0]
inc=dedup_sum('income',2025); exp=dedup_sum('expenses',2025)
loc=80000000
print(f" 2025 total INCOME (firm fees)      = ${inc:,.0f}")
print(f" 2025 total EXPENSES (in-house)     = ${exp:,.0f}")
print(f" 2025 total ACTIVITY (inc+exp)      = ${inc+exp:,.0f}")
print(f" Loc $80M as % of INCOME only       = {100*loc/inc:.2f}%")
print(f" Loc $80M as % of TOTAL activity    = {100*loc/(inc+exp):.2f}%")

print("\n############ CLAIM 4: blank-filings rate — count properly ############")
print(" 2025 filings by presence of a dollar figure:")
r=c.execute("""select
   count(*) total,
   sum(case when income is null and expenses is null then 1 else 0 end) both_null,
   sum(case when income is null then 1 else 0 end) inc_null,
   sum(case when (income is not null and income>0) or (expenses is not null and expenses>0) then 1 else 0 end) has_dollar
   from senate_filings where filing_year=2025""").fetchone()
print(f"   total filings                       = {r['total']:,}")
print(f"   income IS NULL                      = {r['inc_null']:,}  ({100*r['inc_null']/r['total']:.0f}%)  <- what we reported")
print(f"   income AND expenses BOTH null       = {r['both_null']:,}  ({100*r['both_null']/r['total']:.0f}%)  <- truly no dollar figure")
print(f"   has any positive dollar figure      = {r['has_dollar']:,}  ({100*r['has_dollar']/r['total']:.0f}%)")
print("\n by filing_type: which types account for the null-income filings?")
for row in c.execute("""select filing_type,
     count(*) n,
     sum(case when income is null and expenses is null then 1 else 0 end) both_null
     from senate_filings where filing_year=2025 group by filing_type order by n desc limit 12"""):
    print(f"   {row['filing_type']:5} n={row['n']:>6,}  both_null={row['both_null']:>6,}")

content.text: I have everything I need to give you a straight verdict on each figure. Several hold up perfectly; two were framed too strongly and need correcting now. Let me write the fact-check pass.

input.content:
# Fact-check pass — every number, how it's counted, and where it's soft

*I went back at each figure adversarially, the way a hostile fact-checker would. Verdicts below:
two figures are rock-solid, one needs a small denominator caveat, one claim must be reworded, and
one (the blank-filings rate) was overstated and I'm correcting it to the honest number now.*

Quick scorecard:

| Figure | Verdict |
|---|---|
| $80 million | **Solid — use as is** (just call it "reported/claimed," not "spent") |
| "Biggest client in America" | **Reword** — true only by one measure; fix the framing |
| Distortion percentage | **Add the denominator** — 2.78% is income-only; all-in it's ~1.3% |
| Blank-filings rate ("a third") | **Overstated — correct it** to ~25% (and ~10% of activity reports) |
| Enforcement numbers | **Solid — sourced to GAO**, with the caveats already noted |

---

## 1. The $80 million — SOLID

**How it's counted:** Loc Nation filed a Senate report for each quarter of 2025 stating $20,000,000
of income. Counting **one figure per quarter** (the amendment-aware method — take the latest/highest
value per quarter so amendments aren't double-counted): 4 × $20,000,000 = **$80,000,000**. The entity
reports **no** "expenses" figure on any filing, so there's no alternate number in play.

**Could it be challenged?** Only on wording. The raw file, if you naïvely sum every amendment and the
mirrored House copies, reaches $180M — so anyone who wants to attack us would say "$180M is inflated."
We already avoided that by using the conservative **$80M**. The one discipline to keep: always say the
entity **"reported"** or **"claimed"** $80M, never that it **"spent"** or **"was paid"** $80M — no
money changed hands; it's a self-declared figure. **Verdict: stands. Frame as reported/claimed.**

---

## 2. "The biggest lobbying client in America" — REWORD (this one would get caught)

**How it's counted / the problem:** Lobbying filings carry two different dollar fields — **income**
(fees a client pays an outside lobbying firm) and **expenses** (what an organization spends lobbying
**in-house**). Loc Nation is #1 *only by income*. When you look at **in-house expenses**, real
organizations are in the same range or bigger:

- By **income**, 2025: Loc Nation **$80.0M**, then AIPAC $3.76M, Nippon Steel $3.42M. (Loc is ~21×
  the next-largest — that part is real and striking.)
- By **expenses**, 2025: **U.S. Chamber of Commerce $70.3M**, National Association of Realtors
  $54.1M, PhRMA $37.9M, Business Roundtable $33.5M, Meta $26.3M.

So a flat "biggest lobbying client in America" is **not defensible** — the Chamber and others spend
comparable **real** money. **The honest, still-powerful version:** *"By reported income — the fee a
client pays outside lobbying firms — the fabricated entity outranks every real company and trade
group in the country, more than 20 times the next-largest. Its claimed $80 million even exceeds the
single largest real lobbying budget in America, the U.S. Chamber of Commerce's roughly $70 million."*
That's checkable and lands harder because the comparison is to a real heavyweight. **Verdict: drop
"biggest client"; use the income-rank + Chamber comparison instead.**

---

## 3. The distortion percentage — ADD THE DENOMINATOR

**How it's counted:** Loc's $80M over total 2025 reported dollars. But "total" depends on which field:

- Total **income** (firm fees), 2025 = **$2.88B** → Loc = **2.78%**.
- Total **expenses** (in-house), 2025 = **$3.37B**.
- Total **activity** (income + expenses), 2025 = **$6.25B** → Loc = **1.28%**.

**Could it be challenged?** Yes, two ways. (a) "2.78% of all lobbying" is wrong — it's 2.78% of the
*income* slice only; of **all** reported lobbying dollars it's **1.28%**. (b) These totals are my own
computation from the corpus with an amendment-dedup rule, and they run higher than commonly published
figures (e.g., OpenSecrets reports ~$4–4.5B/yr using its own dedup) — so I should **not** present a
precise percentage as if it were an official statistic. **The honest version:** *"one fabricated
filer accounts for well over 1% of all reported federal lobbying dollars in 2025 — about 1.3% of the
income-plus-expenses total, or 2.8% if you look only at the client-fee side — on figures computed
from the raw filings."* Lead with the conservative **~1.3%**. **Verdict: keep, but state the
denominator and lead with the all-in number.**

---

## 4. The blank-filings rate — OVERSTATED; CORRECTING NOW

This is the one we were saying too strongly. We reported **"a third (34–36%) of filings are blank."**

**What that 35% actually is:** it counts filings where the **income** field is null. But in-house
lobbyists legitimately fill in **expenses instead of income** — so an "income-null" filing often
isn't blank at all. Counting filings with **no dollar figure in *either* field**:

- Truly-blank (income **and** expenses both null): **~25%** each year (24–26%, 2022–2025) — not 35%.
- And a big share of *that* is **legitimately** dollarless: **registrations (RR)** — ~6,900 filings a
  year — carry no dollar amount by design, and "no-activity" quarterly reports legitimately report
  none either.
- If you look only at **quarterly activity reports (Q1–Q4)**, the share reporting **neither** income
  nor expenses is about **10–12%** every year (2025: 10%).

**Honest multi-year table (recomputed):**

| Year | income-null (what we said) | both-null (truly no figure) | activity-reports-only, both-null |
|---|---|---|---|
| 2022 | 36% | 26% | 12% |
| 2023 | 36% | 25% | 11% |
| 2024 | 35% | 24% | 11% |
| 2025 | 35% | 25% | 10% |

**Verdict:** the "a third of the ledger is blank" line is **not defensible** and should be dropped.
The honest, still-useful statement: *"About a quarter of all filings carry no dollar figure at all —
though some of that is legitimate (registrations and no-activity reports). Even among quarterly
activity reports, roughly one in ten (10–12%) report neither income nor expenses — and that rate is
strikingly stable year to year."* The **stability** is the real point, and it survives. But note this
is now a **supporting** detail, not a pillar — the two solid pillars of the "no gatekeeper" story are
(a) the fabrication itself sailing through, and (b) the enforcement record.

---

## 5. The enforcement numbers — SOLID (sourced to GAO), historical examples flagged

**How they're counted:** these come straight from the government's own auditor, **GAO-25-107523
(2024 Lobbying Disclosure audit)**, quoting U.S. Attorney's Office data — I read the report directly:

- **3,566** referrals for failing to file LD-2 reports + **2,000** for failing to file LD-203 reports,
  cumulatively **2015–2024** — all for **non-filing**. *(GAO Figures 11–12.)*
- GAO's own wording: enforcement actions "**include letters, emails, and telephone calls**"; **36%**
  (1,290) of LD-2 referrals came into compliance, **63%** (2,244) still pending.
- **2024:** exactly **one** civil action — a **$65,000** settlement against a "chronic offender" —
  and **zero** criminal actions.
- **No** JACK Act prosecutions ever.

**Could it be challenged?** The primary 2015–2024 figures and the 2024 snapshot are airtight (direct
GAO quotes). Two disciplines: (1) the **pre-2015 settlement examples** ($45k/2011, $200k/2013,
$125k/2015) are from older reports and law-firm write-ups — **verify each against its GAO source
before printing a dollar figure**. (2) "None ever for false content" is a **negative** — say "no
documented case," and confirm via DOJ's **§ 1605(b)** semiannual reports and on the record with USAO.
**Verdict: stands, with those two caveats already in the enforcement memo.**

---

## Net effect on the story

Nothing here breaks the story — but it makes it **tighter and unimpeachable**:

- **Keep, unchanged:** the $80M fabrication (reported), that it's ~21× the largest real client by
  income and tops even the U.S. Chamber's ~$70M real budget, and the GAO enforcement record.
- **Reword:** "biggest client in America" → the income-rank + Chamber comparison.
- **Correct:** distortion is ~1.3% all-in (not 2.78% of everything); blank rate is ~25% (with much of
  it legitimate) and ~10% among activity reports — not "a third."
- **Reframe:** the blank-data point drops from a pillar to a supporting detail; the story now rests on
  the two things that can't be argued down — the fabrication got in, and almost nothing happens after.

The two headline sentences I'd stand behind after this pass:

> A single filer registered a fictitious "sovereign nation" and reported **$80 million** in federal
> lobbying fees for 2025 — more than 20 times any real client by that measure, and more than the
> largest real in-house lobbying budget in the country — and it posted to the official record with
> nothing checking it.

> The government's own auditor shows the disclosure law is enforced almost entirely against people who
> **fail to file**, mostly with letters and calls — a single $65,000 civil settlement in 2024, no
> criminal prosecutions — with **no documented case** of anyone ever penalized for filing a false
> figure.

---

### Reproduce any of this
All figures recomputed from the built store over `data/senate/<year>/filings/filings_<year>.json`:
$80M = 4 quarters × max $20M/quarter (client_id 61287); income/expense top-client tables and totals
via amendment-aware dedup (max per registrant+client+period); blank-rate via null-income vs
null-both, by filing_type. Enforcement figures: GAO-25-107523 (https://www.gao.gov/products/gao-25-107523).

# Fact-check pass — every number, how it's counted, and where it's soft

*I went back at each figure adversarially, the way a hostile fact-checker would. Verdicts below:
two figures are rock-solid, one needs a small denominator caveat, one claim must be reworded, and
one (the blank-filings rate) was overstated and I'm correcting it to the honest number now.*

Quick scorecard:

| Figure | Verdict |
|---|---|
| $80 million | **Solid — use as is** (just call it "reported/claimed," not "spent") |
| "Biggest client in America" | **Reword** — true only by one measure; fix the framing |
| Distortion percentage | **Add the denominator** — 2.78% is income-only; all-in it's ~1.3% |
| Blank-filings rate ("a third") | **Overstated — correct it** to ~25% (and ~10% of activity reports) |
| Enforcement numbers | **Solid — sourced to GAO**, with the caveats already noted |

---

## 1. The $80 million — SOLID

**How it's counted:** Loc Nation filed a Senate report for each quarter of 2025 stating $20,000,000
of income. Counting **one figure per quarter** (the amendment-aware method — take the latest/highest
value per quarter so amendments aren't double-counted): 4 × $20,000,000 = **$80,000,000**. The entity
reports **no** "expenses" figure on any filing, so there's no alternate number in play.

**Could it be challenged?** Only on wording. The raw file, if you naïvely sum every amendment and the
mirrored House copies, reaches $180M — so anyone who wants to attack us would say "$180M is inflated."
We already avoided that by using the conservative **$80M**. The one discipline to keep: always say the
entity **"reported"** or **"claimed"** $80M, never that it **"spent"** or **"was paid"** $80M — no
money changed hands; it's a self-declared figure. **Verdict: stands. Frame as reported/claimed.**

---

## 2. "The biggest lobbying client in America" — REWORD (this one would get caught)

**How it's counted / the problem:** Lobbying filings carry two different dollar fields — **income**
(fees a client pays an outside lobbying firm) and **expenses** (what an organization spends lobbying
**in-house**). Loc Nation is #1 *only by income*. When you look at **in-house expenses**, real
organizations are in the same range or bigger:

- By **income**, 2025: Loc Nation **$80.0M**, then AIPAC $3.76M, Nippon Steel $3.42M. (Loc is ~21×
  the next-largest — that part is real and striking.)
- By **expenses**, 2025: **U.S. Chamber of Commerce $70.3M**, National Association of Realtors
  $54.1M, PhRMA $37.9M, Business Roundtable $33.5M, Meta $26.3M.

So a flat "biggest lobbying client in America" is **not defensible** — the Chamber and others spend
comparable **real** money. **The honest, still-powerful version:** *"By reported income — the fee a
client pays outside lobbying firms — the fabricated entity outranks every real company and trade
group in the country, more than 20 times the next-largest. Its claimed $80 million even exceeds the
single largest real lobbying budget in America, the U.S. Chamber of Commerce's roughly $70 million."*
That's checkable and lands harder because the comparison is to a real heavyweight. **Verdict: drop
"biggest client"; use the income-rank + Chamber comparison instead.**

---

## 3. The distortion percentage — ADD THE DENOMINATOR

**How it's counted:** Loc's $80M over total 2025 reported dollars. But "total" depends on which field:

- Total **income** (firm fees), 2025 = **$2.88B** → Loc = **2.78%**.
- Total **expenses** (in-house), 2025 = **$3.37B**.
- Total **activity** (income + expenses), 2025 = **$6.25B** → Loc = **1.28%**.

**Could it be challenged?** Yes, two ways. (a) "2.78% of all lobbying" is wrong — it's 2.78% of the
*income* slice only; of **all** reported lobbying dollars it's **1.28%**. (b) These totals are my own
computation from the corpus with an amendment-dedup rule, and they run higher than commonly published
figures (e.g., OpenSecrets reports ~$4–4.5B/yr using its own dedup) — so I should **not** present a
precise percentage as if it were an official statistic. **The honest version:** *"one fabricated
filer accounts for well over 1% of all reported federal lobbying dollars in 2025 — about 1.3% of the
income-plus-expenses total, or 2.8% if you look only at the client-fee side — on figures computed
from the raw filings."* Lead with the conservative **~1.3%**. **Verdict: keep, but state the
denominator and lead with the all-in number.**

---

## 4. The blank-filings rate — OVERSTATED; CORRECTING NOW

This is the one we were saying too strongly. We reported **"a third (34–36%) of filings are blank."**

**What that 35% actually is:** it counts filings where the **income** field is null. But in-house
lobbyists legitimately fill in **expenses instead of income** — so an "income-null" filing often
isn't blank at all. Counting filings with **no dollar figure in *either* field**:

- Truly-blank (income **and** expenses both null): **~25%** each year (24–26%, 2022–2025) — not 35%.
- And a big share of *that* is **legitimately** dollarless: **registrations (RR)** — ~6,900 filings a
  year — carry no dollar amount by design, and "no-activity" quarterly reports legitimately report
  none either.
- If you look only at **quarterly activity reports (Q1–Q4)**, the share reporting **neither** income
  nor expenses is about **10–12%** every year (2025: 10%).

**Honest multi-year table (recomputed):**

| Year | income-null (what we said) | both-null (truly no figure) | activity-reports-only, both-null |
|---|---|---|---|
| 2022 | 36% | 26% | 12% |
| 2023 | 36% | 25% | 11% |
| 2024 | 35% | 24% | 11% |
| 2025 | 35% | 25% | 10% |

**Verdict:** the "a third of the ledger is blank" line is **not defensible** and should be dropped.
The honest, still-useful statement: *"About a quarter of all filings carry no dollar figure at all —
though some of that is legitimate (registrations and no-activity reports). Even among quarterly
activity reports, roughly one in ten (10–12%) report neither income nor expenses — and that rate is
strikingly stable year to year."* The **stability** is the real point, and it survives. But note this
is now a **supporting** detail, not a pillar — the two solid pillars of the "no gatekeeper" story are
(a) the fabrication itself sailing through, and (b) the enforcement record.

---

## 5. The enforcement numbers — SOLID (sourced to GAO), historical examples flagged

**How they're counted:** these come straight from the government's own auditor, **GAO-25-107523
(2024 Lobbying Disclosure audit)**, quoting U.S. Attorney's Office data — I read the report directly:

- **3,566** referrals for failing to file LD-2 reports + **2,000** for failing to file LD-203 reports,
  cumulatively **2015–2024** — all for **non-filing**. *(GAO Figures 11–12.)*
- GAO's own wording: enforcement actions "**include letters, emails, and telephone calls**"; **36%**
  (1,290) of LD-2 referrals came into compliance, **63%** (2,244) still pending.
- **2024:** exactly **one** civil action — a **$65,000** settlement against a "chronic offender" —
  and **zero** criminal actions.
- **No** JACK Act prosecutions ever.

**Could it be challenged?** The primary 2015–2024 figures and the 2024 snapshot are airtight (direct
GAO quotes). Two disciplines: (1) the **pre-2015 settlement examples** ($45k/2011, $200k/2013,
$125k/2015) are from older reports and law-firm write-ups — **verify each against its GAO source
before printing a dollar figure**. (2) "None ever for false content" is a **negative** — say "no
documented case," and confirm via DOJ's **§ 1605(b)** semiannual reports and on the record with USAO.
**Verdict: stands, with those two caveats already in the enforcement memo.**

---

## Net effect on the story

Nothing here breaks the story — but it makes it **tighter and unimpeachable**:

- **Keep, unchanged:** the $80M fabrication (reported), that it's ~21× the largest real client by
  income and tops even the U.S. Chamber's ~$70M real budget, and the GAO enforcement record.
- **Reword:** "biggest client in America" → the income-rank + Chamber comparison.
- **Correct:** distortion is ~1.3% all-in (not 2.78% of everything); blank rate is ~25% (with much of
  it legitimate) and ~10% among activity reports — not "a third."
- **Reframe:** the blank-data point drops from a pillar to a supporting detail; the story now rests on
  the two things that can't be argued down — the fabrication got in, and almost nothing happens after.

The two headline sentences I'd stand behind after this pass:

> A single filer registered a fictitious "sovereign nation" and reported **$80 million** in federal
> lobbying fees for 2025 — more than 20 times any real client by that measure, and more than the
> largest real in-house lobbying budget in the country — and it posted to the official record with
> nothing checking it.

> The government's own auditor shows the disclosure law is enforced almost entirely against people who
> **fail to file**, mostly with letters and calls — a single $65,000 civil settlement in 2024, no
> criminal prosecutions — with **no documented case** of anyone ever penalized for filing a false
> figure.

---

### Reproduce any of this
All figures recomputed from the built store over `data/senate/<year>/filings/filings_<year>.json`:
$80M = 4 quarters × max $20M/quarter (client_id 61287); income/expense top-client tables and totals
via amendment-aware dedup (max per registrant+client+period); blank-rate via null-income vs
null-both, by filing_type. Enforcement figures: GAO-25-107523 (https://www.gao.gov/products/gao-25-107523).

content.text:
I went back at every number adversarially. The full pass is above; here's the honest scorecard. Two figures are bulletproof, one needs a caveat, and **two we've been framing too strongly** — I'd rather fix them now.

**Solid, use as is:**

- **The $80 million.** $20M × four quarters, counted one figure per quarter so amendments don't double-count. Loc reports no "expenses" field, so there's no competing number. Only discipline: say "reported/claimed," never "spent" — no money moved.
- **The enforcement numbers.** Quoted directly from GAO's 2024 audit: 5,566 non-filing referrals over 2015–2024, all for failure to file; enforcement "letters, emails, and telephone calls"; one $65k civil settlement in 2024; zero criminal; no JACK Act prosecutions ever. The pre-2015 settlement dollar amounts are secondary — verify before printing.

**Correct the framing:**

- **"Biggest client in America" — would get caught.** That's true only by *income* (fees to outside firms). By *in-house* spending (the "expenses" field), the U.S. Chamber reports $70M, Realtors $54M, PhRMA $38M. So drop the flat claim. The honest, actually-stronger version: by reported income it's ~21× the next-largest real client, and its fabricated $80M even tops the single biggest *real* lobbying budget in the country (the Chamber's ~$70M).
- **The distortion percentage — state the denominator.** 2.78% is of the *income* slice only. Of all reported lobbying dollars (income + expenses = $6.25B), it's **1.3%**. And these totals are my own computation, higher than published figures like OpenSecrets' ~$4.4B — so lead with the conservative ~1.3% and don't present a precise number as official.

**Overstated — corrected:**

- **The "a third of filings are blank" line.** That 35% counted only the *income* field being empty — but in-house filers legitimately use the *expenses* field instead. Filings with **no dollar figure at all** are ~**25%**, and much of that is legitimate (registrations carry no dollar amount by design; some quarters have no activity). Among actual quarterly activity reports, only about **10%** report neither figure. So "a third of the ledger is blank" isn't defensible. The honest, still-useful point is the *stability* — ~10–12% of activity reports carry no dollar figure every single year. This drops from a pillar to a supporting detail.

Net effect: the story doesn't break — it gets tighter. It now rests on the two things that can't be argued down: **a fabricated $80M filing sailed into the official record with nothing checking it, and the government's own auditor shows almost nothing happens afterward.** The blank-data stat becomes a minor supporting note rather than a headline.

The two headline sentences I'd stand behind post-fact-check are at the bottom of the memo. Want me to now fold all five memos into a single clean draft using these corrected numbers, or keep digging on anything?

input.content:
# The lobbying record has no gatekeeper — findings summary

**Investigation:** what's the strongest newsworthy story in a corpus of US congressional press
releases and Senate + House lobbying-disclosure filings, 2022–2026 Q1.
**Status:** one confirmed finding, verified and fact-checked; ready for reporting-out (comment,
lawyer review) before publication.
**A note on framing:** this is a story about a **system**, not about the individual who filed. The
person may sincerely hold unusual beliefs; nothing here asserts she did anything wrong. Every number
below has been re-derived from the raw data and carries its source.

---

## The story in plain terms

The federal government keeps an official, public ledger of who lobbies Congress and for how much. It
is the record journalists, researchers, and watchdogs rely on to follow the money in Washington. It
runs on an **honour system**: anyone can register online, type in whatever numbers they like, and the
filing publishes immediately — **nobody checks it on the way in.**

In 2025 that gap produced a striking result. A single person registered a fictitious country — the
"State of Loc Nation Global Public Benefit Corporation" — and filed official lobbying reports claiming
**$20 million every quarter, $80 million for the year.** That one fabricated figure is more than
twenty times larger than any real lobbying client's fees, and larger than the biggest real in-house
lobbying budget in the country. It sailed straight into the official database, was copied into the
House's system too, and quietly inflated the national lobbying totals — and **nothing flagged it.**

And almost nothing happens after the fact either. The government's own auditor shows the disclosure
law is enforced overwhelmingly against people who **fail to file at all** — usually with letters and
phone calls — while there is **no documented case of anyone ever being penalised for filing a false
number.** On paper, a knowingly false filing can draw fines or even prison; in practice the machinery
isn't pointed at false content, and it just sits in the record.

So the story isn't "one weird filing." It's that the disclosure system has **no gatekeeper on the way
in and effectively none on the way out** — and this one vivid filing proves it.

---

## The confirmed finding, with the specifics

### 1. A fabricated entity reported $80 million in 2025 lobbying fees

- **What it is.** Client "STATE OF LOC NATION GLOBAL PUBLIC BENEFIT CORPORATION," self-described in the
  filing as an *"Established sovereign govt: drafting formal constitution, laws, negotiating treaties."*
  The registrant ("Loc Community Association") registered to lobby for itself from a Dover, Delaware
  registered-agent address; the sole lobbyist's "covered position" (prior government job) is listed as
  *"Head of State Black USA."* The filing ties the money to bill H.R. 40 (a reparations-study bill), a
  $500-quadrillion damages claim, and a demand that the Bureau of Engraving & Printing print a new
  currency.
  *Source: `data/senate/2025/filings/filings_2025.json#4d5b1cb0-0971-43b8-958b-d260e2be8af1`;
  live public copy: https://lda.senate.gov/filings/public/filing/4d5b1cb0-0971-43b8-958b-d260e2be8af1/print/*

- **The $80 million — how counted.** $20,000,000 reported for each of the four quarters of 2025,
  counted **one figure per quarter** (amendment-aware, so amendments aren't double-counted): 4 × $20M =
  **$80M**. The entity reports no "expenses" figure, so there is no competing number.
  *Always describe this as "reported/claimed," not "spent" — no money changed hands.*
  *(A naïve sum of every amendment plus the mirrored House copies reaches $180M; we deliberately use the
  conservative $80M.)*
  *Source: the four quarterly filings for client_id 61287, 2025; registration
  `data/senate/2024/filings/filings_2024.json#5000632b-e40b-4762-affc-551b46a40c91`.*

### 2. It dwarfs every real client by the fee measure — and tops the biggest real budget

- By **reported income** (the fee a client pays outside lobbying firms), 2025: Loc Nation **$80.0M**,
  then the next-largest real clients are AIPAC $3.76M and Nippon Steel $3.42M. Loc is **~21× the
  next-largest.**
- Its claimed $80M even **exceeds the single largest *real* lobbying budget in the country** — the U.S.
  Chamber of Commerce's **$70.3M** in reported in-house **expenses** (next: National Association of
  Realtors $54.1M, PhRMA $37.9M).
- **Honest wording caveat:** do **not** call it flatly "the biggest lobbying client in America." That's
  true only by the income measure; large organizations that lobby in-house report comparable *real*
  money in the separate "expenses" field. The defensible claim is the income-rank (~21×) plus the
  Chamber comparison.
  *Source: amendment-aware client rankings by income and by expenses, 2025 Senate filings.*

- As a **single filing**, the $20M quarterly figure is ~**6× the largest legitimate single filing** in
  four years of data (the next-biggest is $3.5M, Industrial Energy Consumers of America, 2023).
  *Source: max single-filing income scan, 2022–2025.*

### 3. It measurably distorted the national totals

- Loc's $80M is **~1.3% of all reported federal lobbying dollars in 2025** (income + expenses =
  $6.25B), or **2.78%** if you look only at the client-fee **income** side ($2.88B).
- **Honest caveat:** these totals are computed from the raw corpus with an amendment-dedup rule and run
  higher than commonly published figures (e.g., OpenSecrets ~$4–4.5B/yr, different dedup) — so present
  the percentage with its denominator stated, and **lead with the conservative ~1.3%**, not as an
  official statistic.
  *Source: amendment-aware sums of income and expenses, 2025 Senate filings.*

### 4. The "no gatekeeper on the way in" is structural, not a one-year blip

- The share of filings carrying **no dollar figure at all** (neither income nor expenses) is stable at
  **~24–26% every year, 2022–2025.** Among **quarterly activity reports specifically**, about
  **10–12% every year** report neither figure — a strikingly consistent rate.
- **Honest caveat / correction:** an earlier draft said "a third of filings are blank." That over-counted
  — it counted only the income field, but in-house filers legitimately use the expenses field instead,
  and registrations/no-activity reports legitimately carry no dollar amount. The corrected figures above
  are the ones to use, and this is a **supporting** detail, not a pillar.
  *Source: null-income vs null-both-fields counts by filing_type and year, 2022–2025 Senate filings.*

### 5. There is essentially no consequence for a false filing — "illegal in principle, unenforced"

- **The law does have teeth on paper.** A *knowing* false lobbying filing can draw a civil fine up to
  **$200,000** (2 U.S.C. § 1606(a)); a *knowing and corrupt* one, up to **5 years** in prison
  (§ 1606(b)); and the general false-statements law (18 U.S.C. § 1001) may also reach it.
  *Sources: https://www.law.cornell.edu/uscode/text/2/1606 · https://www.law.cornell.edu/uscode/text/2/1605 ·
  https://www.law.cornell.edu/uscode/text/18/1001 (read 2026-07-15).*
- **In practice it's aimed at non-filers, not false content.** Per the government's own auditor
  (GAO-25-107523, *2024 Lobbying Disclosure* audit), over **2015–2024** the U.S. Attorney received
  **3,566** referrals for failing to file activity reports and **2,000** for failing to file
  contribution reports — **all for non-filing.** Enforcement actions "**include letters, emails, and
  telephone calls**"; **63%** of referrals were still pending. In all of **2024** DOJ took **one** civil
  action (a **$65,000** settlement against a repeat non-filer) and **zero** criminal actions; there have
  been **no** JACK Act prosecutions ever. GAO records **no** case of enforcement for a false figure.
  *Source: https://www.gao.gov/products/gao-25-107523 (read in full).*
- **The intent catch — and why it points at the system, not the filer.** Every legal hook requires a
  culpable state of mind ("knowingly," "corruptly," "willfully") — i.e., intent to deceive. A filer who
  *sincerely* believes the money is owed may fall outside these statutes entirely. So if she filed
  sincerely, the law may not treat her as a wrongdoer at all — yet the false $80M still entered the
  record and still distorted the totals. Either way, the failure is the **absent gatekeeper**, not the
  individual.

---

## What is confirmed vs. what still needs reporting-out

**Confirmed from primary records (each traceable above):** the filings exist and are live on
lda.senate.gov; the $80M (4 × $20M, amendment-aware); the single self-filer; the sovereign-nation
content; that Loc is ~21× the next client by income and above the Chamber's real budget; the ~1.3%
distortion; the ~10–12% blank-rate among activity reports and its year-to-year stability; the statutory
penalties; and the GAO enforcement figures.

**Still to do before publishing (leads / verification, not yet facts):**
1. **Seek comment** from every named party — including the filer — and treat her fairly as a person who
   may sincerely hold unusual beliefs.
2. **Confirm the enforcement reality on the record:** interview the Secretary of the Senate's Office of
   Public Records / House Clerk (is there any content review?) and obtain DOJ's semiannual **§ 1605(b)**
   enforcement reports — the authoritative all-time count.
3. **Lawyer review** of whether 18 U.S.C. § 1001(c) actually reaches LDA filings, and of any wording
   that implies wrongdoing.
4. **Re-verify** the pre-2015 settlement dollar amounts against their original GAO reports before
   printing them.
5. Optionally extend the blank-rate check to the **House** dataset and pre-2022 years to show the gap is
   long-standing beyond this corpus.

**Guardrails:** the strongest legal statement the records support is *possible, for a lawyer* — never
"X broke the law." No party has been contacted yet, so no "declined to comment" language. Keep the
spotlight on the system.

---

## The two sentences to stand behind

> A single filer registered a fictitious "sovereign nation" and reported **$80 million** in federal
> lobbying fees for 2025 — more than 20 times any real client by that measure, and more than the largest
> real in-house lobbying budget in the country — and it posted to the official public record with nothing
> checking it.

> The government's own auditor shows the disclosure law is enforced almost entirely against people who
> **fail to file**, mostly with letters and calls — a single $65,000 civil settlement in 2024, no
> criminal prosecutions — with **no documented case** of anyone ever penalised for filing a false figure.

---

### Source index
- Fabricated filing (Q1 2025): `data/senate/2025/filings/filings_2025.json#4d5b1cb0-0971-43b8-958b-d260e2be8af1` · public: https://lda.senate.gov/filings/public/filing/4d5b1cb0-0971-43b8-958b-d260e2be8af1/print/
- Original registration (2024): `data/senate/2024/filings/filings_2024.json#5000632b-e40b-4762-affc-551b46a40c91`
- House mirror (senateID 401108853-61287): `house/2025_1stQuarter_XML/301694892.xml`
- Rankings, totals, blank-rate: computed from `data/senate/<year>/filings/filings_<year>.json` (amendment-aware dedup; methods stated inline)
- Law: 2 U.S.C. § 1606, § 1605; 18 U.S.C. § 1001 — law.cornell.edu (read 2026-07-15)
